one_line_takeaway: A training-free, CPU-only detector that reads a web-agent screenshot's gradient statistics and dual-view OCR cues to catch visual prompt injections at ~8x lower latency than a GPT-4o prompt, trading a modest F1 edge for large efficiency gains but still missing about a third of attacks.
key_finding: On WAInjectBench, SnapGuard attains average F1 0.75 (TPR 0.66, FPR 0.09) in 1.81s/image with zero GPU memory overhead (CPU-only), versus GPT-4o-prompt's F1 0.71 (TPR 0.55, FPR 0.00) at 14.50s/image -- roughly 8x faster for a small F1 gain. It substantially beats the other image baselines (Embedding-I F1 0.52, LLaVA-1.5-7B-FT 0.23, JailGuard 0.12, raw LLaVA-1.5-7B 0.00) and all text-based baselines (best is PromptArmor at F1 0.45). Ablation shows the action-oriented pattern detector (APD) is the decisive component (removing it drops F1 to 0.56 and raises FPR to 0.18), while VSI mainly supports recall (removing it drops TPR 0.66->0.49, F1 to 0.56) and contrast-polarity reversal adds a smaller lift (F1 0.64 without it). Text extraction dominates runtime (CPR+OCR = 1.788s, 98.8% of total; VSI 0.021s, APD 0.001s).
core_contribution: SnapGuard is a lightweight, training-free detector that flags prompt-injection attacks against screenshot-based web agents (e.g. Anthropic Computer-Use, OpenAI Browser-Use, SeeAct, UI-TARS) directly from a rendered webpage screenshot, without invoking a large VLM for full-page semantic understanding. Its own novel machinery is two complementary, hand-designed signals fused by a rule-based decision module: (1) a Visual Stability Indicator (VSI) that computes the variance of grayscale spatial-gradient magnitudes across the image (O(HW), no learnable parameters) and flags a screenshot as suspicious when this structural-heterogeneity score falls below a threshold tau, on the observation that injected/malicious visual content suppresses local structural variation and yields abnormally low VSI; and (2) a textual branch that applies 'contrast-polarity reversal' (selectively inverting near-white regions, grayscale threshold gamma=240) as preprocessing, runs lightweight OCR (Pytesseract) on both the original and reversed views, merges the OCR outputs, and matches the merged text against a taxonomy of action-oriented cues (interaction triggers, credential requests, link invitations, control-override instructions) rather than exact keywords or LLM semantics. The paper frames this as reformulating injection detection as multimodal representation analysis over screenshots.
threat_model: Attacker embeds malicious visual/textual content into a rendered webpage so that a screenshot-based web agent, which perceives only the rendered image (no HTML/DOM), executes an attacker-specified action (click malicious link, leak credentials, obey an override instruction) instead of the user's intended task. The paper models two attacker capabilities: a malicious content provider who injects crafted content into user-generated/third-party images (e.g. an adversarial e-commerce seller's product image), and a malicious website owner who controls the full rendered page; both may iteratively refine content against surrogate agents. The defender is an external pre-action module that sees only the raw screenshot (no structured text, no user instruction, no knowledge of attack strategy), must not modify the agent policy, and must add minimal latency/memory. It may optionally use OCR/VLMs to recover text but assumes no HTML access.
limitations: (1) The headline win over GPT-4o-prompt is marginal on F1 (0.75 vs 0.71) and the real advantage is latency/memory, not accuracy; GPT-4o keeps a perfect 0.00 FPR while SnapGuard raises false alarms to 0.09. (2) Absolute detection is modest -- SnapGuard's mean TPR of 0.66 means ~1/3 of attacks slip through, and it is weak on specific attacks (VWA-shot TPR 0.13, WebInject 0.53, Pop-up 0.70). (3) Both core signals are heuristic and threshold-based (low-gradient-variance assumption; a fixed regex/pattern taxonomy in Appendix B.3), so an adaptive attacker who adds local texture to keep VSI high or phrases instructions outside the cue taxonomy could plausibly evade -- no adaptive/white-box evasion against SnapGuard itself is evaluated. (4) SnapGuard is detection-only (block before action); mitigation/recovery is left to future work. (5) Text-based baselines were disadvantaged by an expensive OCR front-end (DeepSeek-OCR: 78.69s, 7.9GB) whose cost is excluded from their reported table figures, and the comparison depends on WAInjectBench's specific attack/sample mix.

datasets_used: WAInjectBench benchmark: 948 benign samples (embedded-image and screenshot categories) and 2,185 malicious samples from eight attack configurations
COCO 2017 validation set: 1,000 benign images (only for training the Embedding-I and LLaVA-1.5-7B-FT baselines)
JailGuard: 1,000 malicious samples (only for training-based baselines)
Eight attacks evaluated: EIA, WebInject, Pop-up, WASP, VWA-emb, VWA-shot, VPI-BU, VPI-CU
quantitative_results: SnapGuard: F1 0.75, TPR 0.66, FPR 0.09, 1.81s/image, 0 MB GPU overhead (CPU-only)
Best text-based baseline PromptArmor: F1 0.45, TPR 0.29; Embedding-T F1 0.00; KAD F1 0.05; PromptGuard F1 0.13; DataSentinel F1 0.05
ROC AUC: SnapGuard 0.742 vs Embedding-I 0.652; SnapGuard reaches ~0.6 TPR at FPR 0.1 (vs ~0.3 for Embedding-I)
Ablation F1: full 0.75; w/o VSI 0.56 (TPR 0.49, FPR 0.05); w/o CPR 0.64 (TPR 0.57, FPR 0.08); w/o APD 0.56 (TPR 0.37, FPR 0.18)
Per-module runtime: CPR+OCR 1.788s (98.8%), VSI 0.021s (1.2%), APD 0.001s (<0.1%), total 1.810s
Text-extraction interface F1/time: Tesseract-OCR 0.75/1.8s, OCR-Alt 0.75/1.7s, LLaVA-1.5 0.46/1.1s, Qwen3-VL 0.76/16.0s, GPT-4o 0.76/15.6s
Fixed thresholds: benign FPR pinned at alpha=0.05 giving VSI threshold=4,450; contrast-reversal grayscale threshold gamma=240
Robustness: SnapGuard holds ~0.8 F1 under increasing Gaussian noise, highest across all perturbation levels
Excluded OCR front-end cost for text baselines: DeepSeek-OCR 78.69s/image, 7.9 GB peak memory
VSI case-study values: benign 17,360 and 16,822 vs malicious 457 and 2,672
baselines_compared: Image-based: Embedding-I (OpenCLIP ViT-B/32 + logistic regression), JailGuard, LLaVA-1.5-7B, LLaVA-1.5-7B-FT (LoRA fine-tuned), GPT-4o-prompt

Text-based: Embedding-T (all-MiniLM-L6-v2 + logistic regression), KAD (Known-Answer Detection), PromptGuard (Meta Llama Prompt Guard 2), DataSentinel (game-theoretic), PromptArmor (LLM sanitizer); text fed via DeepSeek-OCR extraction
SnapGuard's own OCR is Pytesseract; alternative extractors tested: LLaVA-1.5, Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking, GPT-4o
